data.title: Micron: The Only Chip Stock I'm Buying
data.description:
Amid this year's flight to safety in the stock markets, the safest bet in the tech sector has emerged as chip stocks. After all, most chip manufacturers (especially memory companies) are sold out, thanks to the data center boom.

That guarantee of solid revenue growth throughout the next few years has caused a skyrocketing in the semiconductor industry, leaving most companies to be incredibly overvalued. To me, there is one exception: Micron (MU), who, alongside Samsung, SK Hynix, and Western Digital (WDC), are the world's suppliers of NAND memory. Shares of Micron have leaped ~5x over the past year, but the stock is down ~20% since reporting Q2 results in mid-March. In my view, the market's recent profit-taking is a great time to add this stock on recent weakness.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Micron in January, when the stock was trading in the $340s. Since then, Micron briefly spiked to above $460 and has quickly ratcheted down since the company's mid-March earnings release, which I view to be a "buy the rumor, sell the news" pattern on very strong fiscal Q2 (February quarter) results. With an opportunity to buy Micron on this breather, I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating here.

Strong Near-Term Demand Coupled With Longer-Term Contracts

It's impossible to discuss Micron without immediately addressing the company's sharp revenue growth rates, which underscore how rampant demand is for its memory chips. The chart below showcases Micron's revenue by business unit:

Overall, Micron's total revenue of $23.86 billion increased by a whopping 196% y/y, or tripled y/y. Even though supply is constrained (and as such, Micron itself should have high visibility into what its near-term revenue should be), Micron beat Wall Street's revenue expectations of $19.76 billion (+145% y/y) by a wide margin.

What we are most interested in dissecting, however, is the fact that Micron's demand isn't all lopsided. Yes, it's true that Micron notes that demand from data centers is expected to reach 50% of industry TAM in 2026. But this isn't the entire story for Micron. Though a smaller slice of overall company revenue, we note that memory sales to mobile and client (PC) devices rose 245% y/y, while sales to the automotive industry also grew 162% y/y. Memory supply constraints are turning all end markets into voracious buyers and giving Micron the benefit of incredibly elevated pricing (more details in the next section).

For calendar 2026, as shown in the chart below, Micron still expects both DRAM and NAND chips to be fully supply-constrained, with conditions "tight" well past 2026. Importantly, Micron notes that it's now expecting DRAM demand to rise in the low 20s this year, slightly higher than "around 20%" in the prior outlook.

The key worry that many investors have about Micron is this: What if we're investing in Micron at the very peak of its demand cycle? Note that in the past, the memory chip industry has been very boom-and-bust-like airline stocks, with suppliers like Micron and Western Digital ratcheting up capacity during boom times and being forced to cut prices and margins when demand slows down.

What has changed in this cycle, however, is the fact that Micron is now seeking much longer customer commitments. In Q2, the company kicked off what it calls "strategic customer agreements." These SCAs are more specific multi-year commitments, and the company notes that in Q2, it signed its first five-year SCA.

This provides Micron critical visibility that there will be demand to absorb supply much further out than the current supply-constrained 2026-2027 window. This is critical as the company has invested heavily into capacity that won't yield supply until 2028. Earlier this year in January, the company acquired 300,000 square feet of manufacturing capacity in Tongluo, China, which is expected to deliver shipments at scale in 2028. Similarly, recent investments into manufacturing in Singapore are expected to deliver HBM supply in calendar 2027 and new NAND supply in 2028.

Micron's new long-term SCAs indicate that the company isn't just guessing at demand in 2028 and beyond to purchase chips from its long-term expansion sites. In short, we applaud Micron's fierce ~3x y/y revenue growth today, but even more importantly, the company is signing on business for the years ahead as well.

Pricing and Margins Are Unlocking Huge Earnings Growth

The other massive tailwind in Micron's favor is that very tight supply conditions in memory have led to a massive bidding-up of prices. As shown in the chart below, in Q2 Micron benefited from a "mid-60%" sequential increase in DRAM pricing, while NAND increased at an even faster pace in the high 70s.

These are massive pricing jumps in a component that many investors had once thought to be a pure commodity. Micron's gross margin in Q2 hit 74.9%, which improved nearly 20 points sequentially versus 56.0% in Q1 and even far exceeded the company's guidance for just a 68.0% gross margin for the quarter (which indicates that Micron benefited from much richer price appreciation in memory than it had been banking on).

The company expects strength to continue well through Q3, where guidance is calling for gross margins to improve six points sequentially to "approximately 81%." Its revenue guidance for Q3 at $33.5 billion (with a ~2% spread on the high and low end of that midpoint), meanwhile, implies an incredible ~260% y/y growth rate - accelerating from Q2.

Micron's EPS in Q2 hit $12.20, growing ~8x y/y and substantially beating Wall Street's expectations of $9.16 with 33% upside.

Valuation and Key Takeaways

Despite its myriad strengths, Micron remains one of the best-value chip stocks. A surge in forward earnings expectations has positioned Micron's stock at a mere 7x forward P/E. Compare this to direct storage/memory peers like Western Digital and Seagate (STX), which are trading above >30x - at a premium even to market darling NVIDIA (NVDA).

In short, Micron still has plenty of room from a valuation perspective to rise further, especially given the strength of its recent results plus the comfort that we have from multi-year SCA agreements ensuring a healthy demand pipeline over a long timeframe. Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Dorchester Minerals: Strong Recent Growth In Oil Sales Volumes
data.description:
Dorchester Minerals (DMLP) reported a second consecutive quarter with a 15% quarter-over-quarter increase in oil sales volumes. This (along with lease bonus income) helped Dorchester increase its distribution by 10% in Q4 2025 despite relatively weak oil prices.

Since I last looked at Dorchester in December 2025, the oil price environment has changed considerably. At an average of $80 WTI oil in 2026 (and with a recent settlement), Dorchester's average quarterly distribution for 2026 may reach around $0.96 per unit. As well, Q2 2026's distribution should end up well over $1 per unit.

I've increased my estimate of Dorchester's value from $29 to $34.50 per unit due to several factors. These include strong near-term oil prices, an increase in modeled oil sales volumes, and an increase in my long-term WTI oil price from $70 to $75.

Q4 2025 Distribution and Sales Volumes

Dorchester's Q4 2025 distribution increased by approximately 10% quarter-over-quarter to $0.755712 per common unit. This increase came despite softening oil prices during the last few months of 2025. Dorchester's Q4 2025 distribution was also boosted by $6.0 million in lease bonus and other income, with over 50% of its full-year lease bonus and other income being recorded in Q4.

As well, Dorchester reported a 15% quarter-over-quarter increase in oil sales volumes (after a 15% quarter-over-quarter increase in Q3 2025). Those increased volumes helped offset the impact of the 7% decline in Dorchester's realized oil price between Q3 2025 and Q4 2025.

Dorchester's Sales Volumes Q2 2024 Q3 2024 Q4 2024 Q1 2025 Q2 2025 Q3 2025 Q4 2025
Royalty natural gas sales (mmcf) 1,275 1,569 1,568 1,482 1,341 1,607 1,702
Royalty oil sales (mbbls) 423 642 535 518 399 493 592
NPI natural gas sales (mmcf) 493 642 510 436 493 581 453
NPI oil sales (mbbls) 131 198 140 136 163 160 162

Dorchester's Colorado acquisition (which closed in late August 2025) helped its Q4 oil sales volumes, but it also appears that there was significant quarter-over-quarter organic growth.

Recent Settlement

Dorchester announced that it had entered into a settlement with unrelated third parties to resolve litigation. This litigation involved Midland County leaseholds owned by Dorchester, and the settlement resulted in Dorchester receiving $15.5 million in proceeds that will be included in the calculation of the April 2026 Net Profits Interest payment.

This is a one-time boost that should increase Dorchester's Q2 2026 distribution by approximately 31 to 32 cents per unit.

Outlook at $80 WTI Oil in 2026

I had previously modeled Dorchester's 2026 results based on 11,325 BOEPD (64% oil) in sales volumes. That was slightly above its Q3 2025 sales volumes.

Dorchester's Q4 2025 sales volumes jumped to around 12,100 BOEPD (68% oil), including a full quarter of contributions from its Colorado acquisition. Dorchester's sales volumes can vary significantly from quarter to quarter, but I am now modeling Dorchester's 2026 sales volumes at 11,750 BOEPD (66% oil).

At an average WTI oil price of $80, Dorchester may generate $204 million in 2026 revenues. Oil strip prices are quite volatile, but 2026 strip prices have ranged from the mid-$70s to mid-$80s recently.

For the purposes of this calculation, I've excluded the settlement mentioned above.

Revenue $ Million
Royalties $168
Net Profits Interest $24
Other (Including Lease Bonus Income) $12
Total Revenues $204

Dorchester would then be projected to generate $171 million in 2026 distributable cash flow.

$ Million
Operating Expenses $20
G&A $13
Total Cash Expenses $33

Thus, I now expect Dorchester to average around $0.88 to $0.89 per unit in quarterly distributions related to 2026. This excludes the $0.31 to $0.32 per unit in settlement payments, which would increase the quarterly average to around $0.96 per unit.

Dorchester's Q2 2026 distribution seems likely to be fairly large. The Q2 cash receipts attributable to its royalty properties would mainly reflect oil sales from March 2026 to May 2026, a period where WTI oil may average $90+ (based on current strip). The Q2 2026 distribution would also benefit from the settlement payment, so it seems likely to be above $1.10 per unit and potentially approaching $1.30 per unit.

Notes on Valuation

I am increasing my estimate of Dorchester's value from $29 per unit to $34.50 per unit. This is based on long-term (after 2026) $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

That $5.50 per unit increase is due to several factors, each of which is relatively similar in terms of the impact on estimated value.

One factor is the much higher 2026 oil prices compared to when I looked at Dorchester in December 2025, leading to strong near-term distribution expectations. In December 2025, WTI oil strip prices were in the mid-to-high $50s. As well, the settlement payment should boost Dorchester's Q2 2026 distribution.

Another factor is my mid-single-digit upward revision in Dorchester's forward sales volumes, reflecting the impact of Dorchester's strong Q4 2025 sales volumes. I am still modeling Dorchester's forward sales volumes at several percent below Q4 2025 levels to be a bit conservative, though.

The final factor is a $5 increase (from $70 to $75) in my long-term oil price expectations, reflecting higher geopolitical risk going forward.

Conclusion

Dorchester Minerals announced a distribution above $0.75 per unit for Q4 2025, benefiting from strong oil sales volume growth and lease bonus income. Going forward, much improved oil prices may allow Dorchester's average quarterly distribution to reach close to $0.90 per unit during 2026, with a recent settlement adding over $0.30 per unit to its Q2 2026 distribution as well.

I've increased my estimate of Dorchester's value from $29 to $34.50 per unit due to a combination of strong oil sales volumes, excellent near-term oil prices, and a $5 per barrel increase in my long-term oil price.
